questions.question: Explain the concept of an Optimal Binary Search Tree. Derive the dynamic programming recurrence for the cost function c[i][j] and the weight function w[i][j].
keyPoints: OBST minimizes expected search cost: sum of p_i * (depth(k_i) + 1) + q_i * (depth(d_i) + 1)
Optimal substructure: subtrees of an OBST are themselves optimal
w[i][j] = w[i][j-1] + p_j + q_j (weight of probability mass)
c[i][j] = min{c[i][r-1] + c[r][j]} + w[i][j] for r = i+1 to j
Base case: c[i][i] = q_i, w[i][i] = q_i
Root table r[i][j] stores the optimal root for reconstructing the tree

questions.question: Given keys k1=10, k2=20, k3=30 with probabilities p1=0.3, p2=0.1, p3=0.2 and dummy key probabilities q0=0.05, q1=0.1, q2=0.15, q3=0.1, construct the Optimal BST using the DP method. Show the cost, weight, and root tables.
keyPoints: Compute w[i][i] = q_i for base cases
Fill weight table: w[i][j] = w[i][j-1] + p_j + q_j
Fill cost table diagonally: c[i][j] = min over r of {c[i][r-1] + c[r][j]} + w[i][j]
Record the optimal root r[i][j] at each step
Construct the final tree from the root table

questions.question: Why can a balanced BST not always be optimal? Explain with an example how access frequencies affect the optimal structure.
keyPoints: A balanced BST minimizes worst-case depth but ignores access frequencies
If one key is accessed 90% of the time, placing it at the root reduces expected cost
Example: keys {1,2,3} with p1=0.9, p2=0.05, p3=0.05 -- optimal tree has 1 at root, not 2
OBST considers both successful and unsuccessful search probabilities
